status: DEV — clinician_review pending; NOT patient-facing; NOT prescribing guidance; NOT a dose source. This is a CITATION REFERENCE REGISTER of dosing findings reported in the primary literature (real PubMed PMID / DOI, retrieval-verified). It is structurally isolated from the PharmCheck engine (no accessor reads it) and can never feed a dose into a clinical decision. Records author to review_status 'draft' and each requires independent clinician verification against the cited source and AMH/TGA before any use.
isolation_note: No PharmDataSource exposes a getDoseEvidence() accessor and the engine never reads this file. The no-dosages-from-the-LLM invariant is untouched: the engine's only dose source remains the firewall/vendor PharmCheck path.
integrity_bar: A record ships only if its citation.identifier resolved to a real article via get_article_metadata during the verify pass. Unverifiable (potentially hallucinated) PMIDs/DOIs are DROPPED, never kept — a hallucinated citation is worse than an empty register.

attestation.statement: Dose-evidence reference register — retrieval-grounded literature findings, real citations only. NOT prescribing guidance, NOT a dose source. Each record requires clinician verification.

records.context: Stroke prevention in nonvalvular atrial fibrillation; empiric off-label reduced dosing in older adults
records.population: Adults aged 65 and older with nonvalvular AF (n=1172; 147 off-label 'underdosed' matched to 139 standard-dose)
records.dose_statement: In a multicentre retrospective cohort, patients given off-label reduced-dose apixaban (i.e. underdosed outside FDA package-insert dose-reduction criteria) versus standard 5 mg twice daily showed no significant difference in stroke (2.7% vs 2.2%), major bleeding (0% vs 0.7%) or clinically relevant non-major bleeding (2.7% vs 1.4%), while all-cause mortality was higher in the off-label reduced-dose group (10.9% vs 1.4%).

citation.title: Off-Label Reduced Dose Apixaban in Older Adults With Atrial Fibrillation and Associated Outcomes.

records.evidence_note: Retrospective observational cohort across 3 academic centres; association only, susceptible to confounding by indication (sicker patients underdosed).

records.context: Distinguishing dose-adjusted (full-intensity) DOAC therapy for stroke prevention in AF from low-intensity dose-reduced regimens

records.context: Acute gout flare — comparison of low-dose versus high-dose oral colchicine regimens
records.population: Adults with an acute gout flare (intent-to-treat n=184; multicentre randomised double-blind placebo-controlled dose-comparison trial, AGREE)
records.dose_statement: In this randomised trial a low-dose oral colchicine regimen (1.8 mg total over 1 hour) achieved >=50% pain reduction at 24 hours in a similar proportion of patients to a high-dose regimen (4.8 mg total over 6 hours) — 37.8% versus 32.7% responders, both superior to placebo — while the low-dose regimen's adverse-event rate was indistinguishable from placebo and the high-dose regimen caused far more diarrhoea (76.9%) and vomiting. Consistent with the APF22 Section D colchicine monograph statement that low-dose colchicine with an extended dosing interval is effective for acute gout with fewer gastrointestinal effects and less risk of fatal toxicity.

records.dose_statement: In the REDUCE noninferiority trial a 5-day course of prednisone 40 mg daily was noninferior to a 14-day course for time to next exacerbation within 180 days (hazard ratio 0.95, 90% CI 0.70-1.29), with no difference in death or lung-function recovery, while cumulative glucocorticoid exposure was much lower in the short-course arm (mean 379 mg versus 793 mg).
